content.text: Shaw announced that Eliza V3 is launching soon, describing it as a full application runtime that runs across all devices and platforms. Key features include integration with social platforms and iMessage, the ability to issue tasks and control tools like Codex and Claude, workflow creation, and the ability to build and monetize applications at runtime. Shaw noted the architecture is fundamentally different from other agent frameworks and that the team has been coding nonstop for approximately six months. An AMA was hosted on April 30 featuring Shaw, binkyfishai, and baogerbao to discuss Botdick and ElizaOS V3.

Token price concerns dominated community discussion throughout the week. Shaw responded directly to complaints, stating that token holders are not investors in the traditional sense and that buybacks are impossible without revenue. He disclosed that he personally received no significant financial gain from ai16z or ElizaOS beyond a basic salary and is now funding V3 development from personal savings. He urged those seeking short-term gains to sell and leave, emphasizing the project's goal is to build revenue-generating products first. Odilitime offered a more measured perspective, suggesting the team should make the roadmap and plan clearer to token holders, and shared a visual roadmap image coordinated for posting in the announcements channel.

Odilitime confirmed that the $elizaOS token is now the default payment method in x402 for the elizaOS and Milady integration, meaning users can bill x402 services with $ELIZA. Shaw also provided a broader partner update indicating the team is almost ready for another major push, with a new app looking good, several partners lined up to whitelabel the project for their own agents, and cloud infrastructure set up to be profitable. Shadow is launching waifu.fun launchpad with four.meme and flap contracts powered by the new Eliza. Shaw is also training a model intended to be given to users essentially for free at a quality level comparable to Sonnet 4.5.

Shaw shared that he integrated ElizaOS into a Unitree robot priced at around 4000 dollars, enabling it to walk around on command. He also shared several Eliza-related trading repositories including plugin-auto-trader, plugin-social-alpha, and the Spartan project. The social alpha plugin is designed to analyze trenches channels to evaluate whether shilled tokens would have been profitable, with the potential to build a social copy trader from it. Community member marianodim shared progress on a spot trader agent built on ElizaOS incorporating SOL, SUI, and five other tokens with multiple trading strategies.

In the coders channel, developer Dawn shared a field report on a failure mode called memory rot observed in long-lived agents after approximately three months of operation. The issue involves retrieval-only memory architectures such as RAG and vector store plus LLM setups, where old facts persist after becoming stale, causing agents to drift from current state without awareness. The proposed fix involves a reconciliation pass with freshness gates on outgoing claims, periodic cross-source diffs, and re-embedding under the current ontology so the system can detect its own staleness proactively.

Scam activity was flagged multiple times across the week. On April 27, a suspicious link targeting a user asking for help was quickly identified, the targeted user was warned, and the scammer was banned. On April 28 and April 29, additional scammers were removed from the coders channel by Odilitime. Community vigilance and moderator response remained active throughout the week. Separately, Odilitime mentioned the Eliza Army steering group and invited community members and partners interested in contributing to reach out.

A major infrastructure and dependency modernization effort was initiated targeting Node.js 24, TypeScript 6, Bun 1.3.13, and React 19.2.5. Progress was temporarily blocked by a missing transitive dependency causing bun install failures, with PR elizaos/eliza#7146 identified as the critical blocker. Once resolved, the team completed upgrades including viem to v2.48.8, tsdown to 0.21.0, and Node.js to v24. AI SDK dependencies were also updated including the Anthropic AI SDK to version 0.92.0 and multiple AI SDK provider utilities. CI/CD infrastructure was refreshed with updates to Supabase Postgres docker tags and GitHub action versions.

The core agent server was decoupled from application-specific packages through migration to a plugin-lifecycle registration system, improving overall modularity. Hard imports of app packages were removed from the agent server and dangling imports from deleted re-export shims were cleaned up. Cloud infrastructure was migrated from Next.js to a Vite SPA with Hono Workers, and image generation storage was transitioned to R2. Vercel AI Gateway was replaced with OpenRouter using OpenAI and Anthropic as failovers.

A new secrets vault package, @elizaos/vault, was launched to standardize secrets management across platforms with an integrated Settings UI. Self-hosted connectivity was expanded to support HTTPS dashboards, Capacitor mobile, and Electrobun desktop builds including secure authentication protocols. A full local agent architecture for Android was implemented including a foreground service for Bun runtime execution. CLI authentication hangs were resolved by implementing auto-clearing for stale Steward and Privy tokens.

Critical build failures were resolved across plugin-ollama, plugin-openai, plugin-openrouter, and plugin-pdf by removing incompatible CJS bundles to align with ESM standards. Plugin-jupiter was successfully migrated to the api.jup.ag endpoint with mandatory API key authentication. A process-level segfault on headless Linux servers was resolved by bypassing the OS keychain when D-Bus is unavailable. Telegram integration bugs including token passing failures, Bun runtime errors on bot.launch(), and dual-polling race conditions were addressed across issues #7240, #7241, and #7245.

Authentication and client architecture were standardized by merging duplicate MiladyClient definitions into a canonical ElizaClient. Authentication reliability was improved by fixing SIWE domain resolution and updating Codex CLI token recognition. The n8n plugin was expanded with TriggerContext for conversation routing and RuntimeContextProvider for improved data handling. Security and credential management were hardened through disconnect-purge functionality and stricter runtime-fact rules. N8n workflow capabilities were further enhanced with structured clarification rules and ID-based directives.

A new NoModelProviderConfiguredError was introduced to give actionable feedback when agents are misconfigured. Telegram read receipt logic was optimized by switching to ID-based fetching for significant performance improvements. Pay-as-you-go container hosting and credit balance management were enabled for direct billing from org earnings. Milady LifeOps was expanded with Google Calendar management capabilities. Two strategic integrations were proposed and finalized: Hive Civilization on Base mainnet and SwarmScore for agent reputation ratings.
